Curriculum → Security, multi-region, and operations

Module overview

Security, multi-region, and operations

Design systems that are safe to expose, recoverable during disasters, and maintainable by real teams over time.

4 lessons 0/4 complete Start module

How to navigate it

Use this module like a guided drill

  • Scan the lesson summaries first so you know the progression before opening the first topic.
  • Use the “why it matters” snippets to prioritize the topics that are most likely to surface in interviews.
  • Save the case-study and practice sections for speaking answers out loud after reading the lesson once.

Objectives

What to master here

  • Apply practical authentication, authorization, and data protection patterns
  • Reason about multi-region architecture and disaster recovery
  • Include deployment, capacity, and cost controls in designs

How to use this module

Practice flow

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Interview signal

What reviewers expect

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson 1

Security foundations for system design

Open

Security choices should appear throughout the design, not as a last-minute box labeled auth.

Why it matters

Interviewers want to see that you protect data, isolate access, and anticipate abuse without derailing the rest of the design.

What you will cover

Authenticate and authorize clearlyProtect data in transit and at restDefend against common abuse

Good interview signal

  • Separate authn from authz.
  • Encrypt transport and sensitive data at rest.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 2

Multi-region design and disaster recovery

Open

Use multiple regions when the business case justifies lower outage risk, lower latency, or data residency needs, while accepting the added coordination cost.

Why it matters

This is a high-signal interview area because it forces you to reconcile availability goals with replication lag, cost, and operational maturity.

What you will cover

Know what you are optimizing forReplication and control planesRecovery is a practiced capability

Good interview signal

  • Define RTO and RPO before proposing multi-region complexity.
  • Choose active-passive or active-active intentionally.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 3

Fault tolerance and graceful degradation

Open

Healthy systems keep the core user journey alive when dependencies fail by shedding optional work and falling back to simpler behavior.

Why it matters

This is how you turn abstract availability goals into concrete runtime behavior.

What you will cover

Design degradable experiencesContain failures earlyTest the degraded mode

Good interview signal

  • Name the minimal user journey that must survive.
  • Shed optional work first during incidents.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 4

Deployments, capacity, and cost awareness

Open

Production systems need safe release mechanics, realistic capacity headroom, and an explicit understanding of what the architecture costs to run.

Why it matters

A design is incomplete if it only works in steady state and ignores change management or economics.

What you will cover

Release safelyPlan headroom and autoscalingCost is part of the trade-off

Good interview signal

  • Mention a release strategy and rollback plan.
  • Reserve capacity for bursts and failover.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
